topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implementation of Binary Search Tree - Creation, Insertion, Deletion, Search # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a node-based binary tree data structure that satisfies the **BST Invariant Property**: for every node $N$ in the tree, all keys in the left subtree of $N$ are **strictly less** than the key of $N$, and all keys in the right subtree of $N$ are **strictly greater** than the key of $N$. The left and right subtrees themselves must also be binary search trees. Formally, for any node $N$ with key $k(N)$: $$\forall \, x \in \text{LeftSubtree}(N) : \text{key}(x) < k(N)$$ $$\forall \, x \in \text{RightSubtree}(N) : \text{key}(x) > k(N)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"A Binary Search Tree is a binary tree where the left child of a node contains only nodes with values lesser than the node's value, and the right child contains only nodes with values greater than the node's value. This property is recursively applied to all subtrees."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dictionary Lookup Intuition Imagine you are searching for the word **"Mango"** in a physical English dictionary. You do not start from page 1 and read every word. Instead: 1. You open the **middle page** of the dictionary. 2. If the word on that page starts with a letter **after 'M'** (e.g., 'P'), you know "Mango" is in the **left half**. 3. You repeat the process, halving the search space each time. A BST works on the **exact same principle** — except instead of alphabetic order, it follows numeric (or any comparable) order. Every comparison **eliminates one entire subtree** from consideration, just like turning a page block away in the dictionary. > [!NOTE] > **Why BSTs Matter in Engineering:** > - Used in **Database Indexing** (B-Trees are a generalized BST). > - Powers **Auto-complete** in search engines and IDEs. > - Foundation for **AVL Trees, Red-Black Trees, and Splay Trees** used in OS process schedulers and memory management. > - Underlies **Huffman Coding** for data compression. ## 1.3 Node Structure (C Implementation) ```c struct Node { int data; // Key value stored in the node struct Node* left; // Pointer to left child (smaller keys) struct Node* right; // Pointer to right child (larger keys) }; ``` The tree is accessed through a single pointer called the **root**, which is of type `struct Node*`. If `root == NULL`, the tree is **empty**. > [!TIP] > **Common Viva Question:** *"What is the difference between a Binary Tree and a Binary Search Tree?"* > **Answer:** Every BST is a Binary Tree, but **not every Binary Tree is a BST**. A generic Binary Tree imposes **no ordering constraint** on node values. A BST enforces the strict left-less / right-greater invariant on every node. A generic Binary Tree can have any arrangement; a BST cannot. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Fundamental BST Operations ### 2.1.1 CREATION (Initialization) Creation refers to initializing an **empty BST** by setting the `root` pointer to `NULL`. No memory is allocated yet — the tree is a logical entity waiting for its first `insert` operation. ``` root = NULL; // Tree is now empty ``` ### 2.1.2 INSERTION Insertion follows a **top-down recursive path**: 1. If the tree is empty, the new node becomes the `root`. 2. If the tree is not empty, compare the new key `k` with the current node's key. 3. If $k < \text{current.key}$ → recurse into the **left subtree**. 4. If $k > \text{current.key}$ → recurse into the **right subtree**. 5. If $k == \text{current.key}$ → **reject** (duplicates not allowed in standard BST). The first `NULL` pointer encountered becomes the parent of the new leaf node. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property:** Insertion **always** creates a **leaf node** — it never modifies existing nodes. ### 2.1.3 SEARCH Search is the **mirror logic** of insertion: 1. Start at `root`. 2. Compare target `k` with current node's key. 3. If $k == \text{current.key}$ → **found**, return pointer. 4. If $k < \text{current.key}$ → go **left**. 5. If $k > \text{current.key}$ → go **right**. 6. If `NULL` reached → **not found**, return `NULL`. ### 2.1.4 DELETION (The Tricky One) Deletion has **three cases** based on the number of children of the target node: | Case | Configuration | Action | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Case 1** | Node has **0 children** (leaf) | Simply **remove** the node by freeing it; set parent's link to `NULL`. | | **Case 2** | Node has **1 child** | **Replace** the node with its only child; free the original node. | | **Case 3** | Node has **2 children** | Find the **inorder successor** (smallest node in right subtree) OR **inorder predecessor** (largest node in left subtree), **copy** its value to the target node, then **recursively delete** that successor/predecessor. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often forget to free memory in Case 3 after copying the successor's value, causing a **memory leak**. Always free the successor node, not the original target. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Property | Formula / Value | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | BST Search Time (Average) | $O(\log n)$ | Balanced BST | | BST Search Time (Worst) | $O(n)$ | Skewed (degenerate) BST | | BST Insert Time (Average) | $O(\log n)$ | One pass from root to leaf | | BST Insert Time (Worst) | $O(n)$ | Skewed tree | | BST Delete Time (Average) | $O(\log n)$ | Includes successor search | | BST Delete Time (Worst) | $O(n)$ | Skewed tree | | Space Complexity | $O(n)$ | $n$ nodes, each with 2 pointers + 1 int | | Height of Balanced BST | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | For $n$ nodes | | Max Nodes for Height $h$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | Full binary tree | | Min Nodes for Height $h$ | $h + 1$ | Skewed tree (essentially a linked list) | | Inorder Traversal of BST | **Sorted ascending order** | This is BST's defining output property | | Total Comparisons (Search Avg) | $\approx \log_2 n$ | Halving the search space | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** In a **generic Binary Tree**, an inorder traversal yields nodes in **insertion/random order**. In a **BST**, an inorder traversal **always** yields nodes in **ascending sorted order**. This is a guaranteed property used in KTU viva questions. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | BST Role | | :--- | :--- | | **Database Indexing** | B-Trees (generalized BSTs) index millions of records for $O(\log n)$ lookups. | | **File Systems** | ext4, NTFS use tree structures to map file paths to disk blocks. | | **Compiler Design** | Symbol tables use BSTs to store identifiers and their attributes. | | **Network Routing** | Longest prefix matching uses modified BSTs (tries). | | **Gaming AI** | Decision trees (BST variant) for NPC behavior selection. | | **Expression Parsing** | Expression trees (a BST variant) evaluate arithmetic expressions. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Walkthrough — Building a BST from Scratch ### 3.1.1 Insertion Sequence: **50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80** We will derive the tree structure step-by-step using the **BST insertion rule**. **Step 1: Insert 50 (First element)** Tree is empty → 50 becomes the **root**. ``` 50 / \ NULL NULL ``` **Step 2: Insert 30** Compare 30 with 50: $30 < 50$ → go **left**. Left child is `NULL` → insert here. $$ \text{30 becomes left child of 50} $$ ``` 50 / \ 30 NULL / \ NULL NULL ``` **Step 3: Insert 70** Compare 70 with 50: $70 > 50$ → go **right**. Right child is `NULL` → insert here. $$ \text{70 becomes right child of 50} $$ ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ NULL NULL NULL NULL ``` **Step 4: Insert 20** Path: $20 < 50$ (left) → $20 < 30$ (left) → `NULL` → insert. ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / 20 ``` **Step 5: Insert 40** Path: $40 < 50$ (left) → $40 > 30$ (right) → `NULL` → insert. ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ 20 40 ``` **Step 6: Insert 60** Path: $60 > 50$ (right) → $60 < 70$ (left) → `NULL` → insert. ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / 20 40 60 ``` **Step 7: Insert 80** Path: $80 > 50$ (right) → $80 > 70$ (right) → `NULL` → insert. $$ \text{Final BST} $$ ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Verification via Inorder Traversal:** Apply Left → Root → Right recursively. > Result: 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 — **perfectly sorted ascending**. This confirms the BST property is preserved. ## 3.2 Deletion Case Analysis — The Three Scenarios ### 3.2.1 Case 1: Delete a Leaf Node (e.g., delete 20) $$ \text{20 is a leaf} \Rightarrow \text{simply set parent(20).left} = NULL $$ **Resulting tree:** ``` 50 / \ 30 70 \ / \ 40 60 80 ``` ### 3.2.2 Case 2: Delete a Node with One Child (e.g., delete 30) Node 30 has only one child: 40 (right). $$ \text{Replace 30 with 40: parent(30) = 50, so 50.left} = 40 $$ **Resulting tree:** ``` 50 / \ 40 70 / \ 60 80 ``` ### 3.2.3 Case 3: Delete a Node with Two Children (e.g., delete 50 — the root) Node 50 has two children (40 and 70). We use the **inorder successor** method: $$ \text{Inorder successor of 50} = \text{smallest node in right subtree of 50} $$ $$ \text{Right subtree of 50 is rooted at 70. Smallest in that subtree} = 60 $$ **Action:** Copy 60's value to node 50, then delete node 60 (which is a leaf — Case 1). $$ \text{50.key} \leftarrow 60, \quad \text{then delete node 60} $$ **Resulting tree:** ``` 60 / \ 40 70 \ 80 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this preserve BST property?** The inorder successor is the **next larger value** in sorted order, so replacing the deleted node's key with the successor's key keeps the tree sorted. The successor is **guaranteed to have at most one child** (it cannot have a left child by definition), so the recursive deletion reduces to Case 1 or Case 2. ## 3.3 Time Complexity Derivation ### 3.3.1 Average Case (Balanced BST) In a balanced BST of height $h$, the number of nodes is: $$ n = 2^{h+1} - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h \approx \log_2 n $$ Each operation (search/insert/delete) traverses **at most one path** from root to leaf. The path length equals the height $h$. $$ T(n) = O(h) = O(\log_2 n) $$ ### 3.3.2 Worst Case (Skewed BST) If insertions happen in **sorted order** (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 40, 50), the BST degenerates into a **linked list**: ``` 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50 ``` The height becomes $h = n - 1$, so: $$ T(n) = O(n) $$ ### 3.3.3 Recurrence Relation For a balanced BST, the time to search satisfies: $$ T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1) $$ Applying the **Master Theorem** with $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = O(1)$: $$ T(n) = O(\log n) $$ ## 3.4 Complete C Program (Reference Implementation) ```c #include #include // ---------- Node Definition ---------- struct Node { int data; struct Node* left; struct Node* right; }; // ---------- Create New Node ---------- struct Node* createNode(int value) { struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); newNode->data = value; newNode->left = NULL; newNode->right = NULL; return newNode; } // ---------- Insert Operation ---------- struct Node* insert(struct Node* root, int value) { if (root == NULL) { return createNode(value); // Base case: empty spot found } if (value < root->data) { root->left = insert(root->left, value); } else if (value > root->data) { root->right = insert(root->right, value); } // else: duplicate, do nothing return root; } // ---------- Search Operation ---------- struct Node* search(struct Node* root, int key) { if (root == NULL || root->data == key) { return root; } if (key < root->data) { return search(root->left, key); } else { return search(root->right, key); } } // ---------- Find Minimum (for Inorder Successor) ---------- struct Node* findMin(struct Node* node) { while (node->left != NULL) { node = node->left; } return node; } // ---------- Delete Operation ---------- struct Node* deleteNode(struct Node* root, int key) { if (root == NULL) return root; if (key < root->data) { root->left = deleteNode(root->left, key); } else if (key > root->data) { root->right = deleteNode(root->right, key); } else { // Node found — apply 3 cases if (root->left == NULL) { struct Node* temp = root->right; free(root); return temp; } else if (root->right == NULL) { struct Node* temp = root->left; free(root); return temp; } // Case 3: Two children struct Node* temp = findMin(root->right); // Inorder successor root->data = temp->data; root->right = deleteNode(root->right, temp->data); } return root; } // ---------- Inorder Traversal ---------- void inorder(struct Node* root) { if (root != NULL) { inorder(root->left); printf("%d ", root->data); inorder(root->right); } } // ---------- Preorder Traversal ---------- void preorder(struct Node* root) { if (root != NULL) { printf("%d ", root->data); preorder(root->left); preorder(root->right); } } // ---------- Postorder Traversal ---------- void postorder(struct Node* root) { if (root != NULL) { postorder(root->left); postorder(root->right); printf("%d ", root->data); } } // ---------- Main Driver ---------- int main() { struct Node* root = NULL; int choice, value; while (1) { printf("\n--- BST MENU ---\n"); printf("1. Insert\n2. Delete\n3. Search\n4. Inorder\n"); printf("5. Preorder\n6. Postorder\n7. Exit\n"); printf("Enter choice: "); scanf("%d", &choice); switch (choice) { case 1: printf("Enter value to insert: "); scanf("%d", &value); root = insert(root, value); break; case 2: printf("Enter value to delete: "); scanf("%d", &value); root = deleteNode(root, value); break; case 3: printf("Enter value to search: "); scanf("%d", &value); if (search(root, value) != NULL) printf("%d FOUND in tree.\n", value); else printf("%d NOT FOUND.\n", value); break; case 4: printf("Inorder: "); inorder(root); printf("\n"); break; case 5: printf("Preorder: "); preorder(root); printf("\n"); break; case 6: printf("Postorder: "); postorder(root); printf("\n"); break; case 7: exit(0); default: printf("Invalid choice.\n"); } } return 0; } ``` ### 3.4.1 Sample Input/Output Trace **Input sequence:** Insert 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80 **Operations and outputs:** | Operation | Output / Result | | :--- | :--- | | Inorder traversal | `20 30 40 50 60 70 80` (sorted ascending) | | Preorder traversal | `50 30 20 40 70 60 80` | | Postorder traversal | `20 40 30 60 80 70 50` | | Search 40 | `40 FOUND in tree.` | | Search 100 | `100 NOT FOUND.` | | Delete 30 (one child) | Inorder: `20 40 50 60 70 80` | | Delete 50 (two children) | Inorder: `20 40 60 70 80` (60 was inorder successor) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BST Structure Diagram — Final Tree After All Insertions ```mermaid graph TD node50["50 ROOT"]:::rootNode node30["30"]:::leftNode node70["70"]:::rightNode node20["20"]:::leafNode node40["40"]:::leafNode node60["60"]:::leafNode node80["80"]:::leafNode null1["NULL"]:::nullNode null2["NULL"]:::nullNode null3["NULL"]:::nullNode null4["NULL"]:::nullNode node50 --> node30 node50 --> node70 node30 --> node20 node30 --> node40 node70 --> node60 node70 --> node80 node20 --> null1 node20 --> null2 node40 --> null3 node40 --> null4 classDef rootNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef leftNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef rightNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef leafNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef nullNode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#808080,stroke-width:1px,color:#696969 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The **gold root (50)** splits into a **blue left subtree (30, 20, 40)** containing values less than 50, and a **salmon right subtree (70, 60, 80)** containing values greater than 50. The **green leaves** (20, 40, 60, 80) have no children. The **gray NULL** pointers terminate every branch. ## 4.2 Operation Flowchart — High-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD startA(["User Calls Operation"]):::entryNode decisionEmpty{"Is root == NULL?"}:::decideNode createRoot["Create Node
Allocate Memory
Set left/right to NULL"]:::processNode compareKey{"key vs root.data"}:::decideNode goLeft["Recurse: root.left"]:::leftFlow goRight["Recurse: root.right"]:::rightFlow checkChildren{"How many children?"}:::decideNode case1["CASE 1: Leaf
Free node
Return NULL"]:::case1Style case2["CASE 2: One Child
Return child pointer"]:::case2Style case3["CASE 3: Two Children
Find Inorder Successor
Copy value
Recursively delete successor"]:::case3Style result(["Return Modified Root"]):::exitNode startA --> decisionEmpty decisionEmpty -- "YES" --> createRoot --> result decisionEmpty -- "NO" --> compareKey compareKey -- "key less than root.data" --> goLeft --> compareKey compareKey -- "key greater than root.data" --> goRight --> compareKey compareKey -- "key equal" --> checkChildren checkChildren -- "0 children" --> case1 --> result checkChildren -- "1 child" --> case2 --> result checkChildren -- "2 children" --> case3 --> result classDef entryNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef exitNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef decideNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef processNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef leftFlow fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef rightFlow fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef case1Style fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef case2Style fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef case3Style fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The **red entry** is the function call. The **yellow diamonds** are decision points. The **green exit** returns the new subtree root. Notice the **recursive back-edge** from `goLeft` and `goRight` to `compareKey` — this is what makes the algorithm traverse down the tree level by level until it finds the correct insertion/deletion spot. ## 4.3 Deletion Case 3 — Step-by-Step Visual ```mermaid graph TD before["BEFORE Delete 50"]:::beforeStyle node50a["50"]:::targetNode node30a["30"]:::leftNode node70a["70"]:::rightNode node40a["40"]:::leafNode node60a["60"]:::successorNode node80a["80"]:::leafNode before --> node50a node50a --> node30a node50a --> node70a node30a --> node40a node70a --> node60a node70a --> node80a classDef beforeStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef targetNode fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef successorNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef leftNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef rightNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef leafNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Transformation:** The **red target (50)** gets replaced by the **gold successor (60)**. Node 60 is then deleted from its original position. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Binary Search Tree. State the BST property formally.** **Model Answer:** A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree data structure in which for every node $N$, all keys in the left subtree of $N$ are strictly less than the key of $N$, and all keys in the right subtree of $N$ are strictly greater than the key of $N$. Formally: $$ \forall \, x \in L(N) : \text{key}(x) < \text{key}(N) $$ $$ \forall \, x \in R(N) : \text{key}(x) > \text{key}(N) $$ where $L(N)$ and $R(N)$ denote the left and right subtrees of $N$. The left and right subtrees must also be BSTs (recursive property). BST enables $O(\log n)$ average-case search, insertion, and deletion. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the time complexity of search operation in a BST? Differentiate between average and worst case.** **Model Answer:** The time complexity of BST search depends on the tree's height $h$. - **Average case (balanced BST):** $O(\log n)$ — the height is $\approx \log_2 n$ and the search traverses one path from root to leaf. - **Worst case (skewed/degenerate BST):** $O(n)$ — when insertions occur in sorted order, the BST degenerates into a linked list of height $n - 1$, requiring linear time. The space complexity is $O(n)$ for storing $n$ nodes. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Write a C function to insert a new node into a Binary Search Tree. Explain the algorithm with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a C function to search for a given key in a BST. Trace the algorithm for searching key = 45 in a BST built from insertions: 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Algorithm (Insertion):** 1. If `root == NULL`, allocate a new node, set its data field to the value, initialize left/right to NULL, and return the new node. 2. If `value < root->data`, recursively call `insert(root->left, value)`. 3. If `value > root->data`, recursively call `insert(root->right, value)`. 4. If `value == root->data`, reject (duplicate handling). 5. Return the (possibly modified) `root`. **C Function:** ```c struct Node* insert(struct Node* root, int value) { if (root == NULL) { struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); newNode->data = value; newNode->left = newNode->right = NULL; return newNode; } if (value < root->data) root->left = insert(root->left, value); else if (value > root->data) root->right = insert(root->right, value); return root; } ``` **Example:** Inserting 35 into the tree built from {50, 30, 70, 20, 40}: - 35 < 50 → go left to 30 - 35 > 30 → go right to 40 - 35 < 40 → go left (NULL) → insert 35 as left child of 40 #### Model Solution for (b): **C Function:** ```c struct Node* search(struct Node* root, int key) { if (root == NULL || root->data == key) return root; if (key < root->data) return search(root->left, key); else return search(root->right, key); } ``` **Trace for key = 45 in BST {50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80}:** | Step | Compare | Decision | Current Node | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | 45 vs 50 | $45 < 50$ | Go left → 30 | | 2 | 45 vs 30 | $45 > 30$ | Go right → 40 | | 3 | 45 vs 40 | $45 > 40$ | Go right → NULL | | 4 | NULL reached | NOT FOUND | Return NULL | **Result:** Key 45 is **NOT FOUND** in the tree. **Time Complexity:** $O(h) = O(\log n)$ for balanced BST. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the three cases of deletion in a Binary Search Tree with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a complete C program to create a BST by inserting elements, perform an inorder traversal, and delete a node with two children. Show the output. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Case 1 — Leaf Node (0 children):** Delete 20 from the tree. Since 20 has no children, we simply free the node and set its parent's left pointer to NULL. This is the simplest case. **Case 2 — Node with One Child:** Delete 30 (which has only right child 40). We replace 30 with 40, effectively bypassing node 30. The parent's pointer (50.left) is updated to point to 40 directly. **Case 3 — Node with Two Children:** Delete 50 (the root, with children 30 and 70). - **Step 1:** Find the **inorder successor** — the smallest value in the right subtree (70). Traversing left from 70 gives 60. - **Step 2:** Copy 60's value to node 50. Now the root holds 60. - **Step 3:** Recursively delete the original node 60 (which is a leaf — Case 1). **Diagrams:** ``` BEFORE Case 3: AFTER Case 3: 50 60 / \ / \ 30 70 40 70 / \ / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 60 80 <-- delete 60 (leaf) ``` #### Model Solution for (b): **Complete C Program:** ```c #include #include struct Node { int data; struct Node* left, *right; }; struct Node* createNode(int v) { struct Node* n = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); n->data = v; n->left = n->right = NULL; return n; } struct Node* insert(struct Node* r, int v) { if (r == NULL) return createNode(v); if (v < r->data) r->left = insert(r->left, v); else if (v > r->data) r->right = insert(r->right, v); return r; } struct Node* findMin(struct Node* n) { while (n->left != NULL) n = n->left; return n; } struct Node* deleteNode(struct Node* r, int key) { if (r == NULL) return r; if (key < r->data) r->left = deleteNode(r->left, key); else if (key > r->data) r->right = deleteNode(r->right, key); else { if (r->left == NULL) { struct Node* t = r->right; free(r); return t; } else if (r->right == NULL) { struct Node* t = r->left; free(r); return t; } struct Node* t = findMin(r->right); r->data = t->data; r->right = deleteNode(r->right, t->data); } return r; } void inorder(struct Node* r) { if (r != NULL) { inorder(r->left); printf("%d ", r->data); inorder(r->right); } } int main() { struct Node* root = NULL; int arr[] = {50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80}; int n = sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]); for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) root = insert(root, arr[i]); printf("Inorder before deletion: "); inorder(root); printf("\n"); root = deleteNode(root, 50); // Delete root with 2 children printf("Inorder after deleting 50: "); inorder(root); printf("\n"); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Inorder before deletion: 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 Inorder after deleting 50: 20 30 40 60 70 80 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to update parent's pointer** after recursive call — your function must do `root->left = deleteNode(root->left, key)`, not just `deleteNode(root->left, key)`. The assignment is **mandatory**. > 2. **Not handling NULL root** — your function will crash on an empty tree. Always check `if (root == NULL) return root;` first. > 3. **In Case 3, deleting the wrong node** — you must copy the successor's **value** to the target and then **recursively delete the successor**, not the original target. > 4. **Ignoring duplicate values** — standard BSTs reject duplicates; failing to handle this explicitly loses 1-2 marks. > 5. **Missing `#include `** for `malloc` — compilation error, full marks lost. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **BST Property:** Left child $< $ Parent $<$ Right child (strict inequality, no duplicates in standard BST). - **Inorder traversal of BST = Sorted output** (most important viva fact). - **Insertion always creates a leaf node** — never modifies existing nodes. - **Deletion has 3 cases:** Leaf (free), One child (bypass), Two children (use inorder successor or predecessor). - **Inorder Successor** = leftmost node in right subtree. - **Inorder Predecessor** = rightmost node in left subtree. - **Time complexity:** $O(\log n)$ average, $O(n)$ worst (skewed). - **Skewed BST = Linked list** — worst case occurs with sorted insertion order. - **Node structure:** 3 fields — `data`, `left` pointer, `right` pointer. - **Memory:** Use `malloc(sizeof(struct Node))` in C; always `free()` deleted nodes to prevent leaks. - **Search returns pointer** to node if found, `NULL` if not found. - **Recursion is the natural approach** for BST operations — base case is `root == NULL`. - **BST vs Binary Tree:** BST has ordering invariant; Binary Tree does not. - **Foundation for:** AVL Trees, Red-Black Trees, B-Trees, Tries. - **Applications:** Database indexing, symbol tables, file systems, autocomplete. - **Height of balanced BST with $n$ nodes:** $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. - **Max nodes in BST of height $h$:** $2^{h+1} - 1$. - **Min nodes in BST of height $h$:** $h + 1$ (skewed). - **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ — one node per data element.

topics.title: Implementation of Tree Traversals - Inorder, Preorder, Postorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Tree Traversals - Inorder, Preorder, Postorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Binary Tree Traversal** is the systematic process of visiting every node in a binary tree data structure **exactly once** in a specific, well-defined order. For KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PCEOL308 – Data Structures Lab**), traversals are classified as **Depth-First Traversals (DFT)**, where the algorithm explores as far as possible down each branch before backtracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module Experiment 2 mandates the **recursive implementation** of all three primary traversals — *Inorder*, *Preorder*, and *Postorder* — on a manually constructed binary tree using the **C programming language** with dynamic memory allocation (`malloc`).

The three standard depth-first traversals are formally defined as:

- **Inorder Traversal (LDR):** Visit **L**eft subtree → Visit **R**oot → Visit **R**ight subtree.
- **Preorder Traversal (DLR):** Visit **D**ata (Root) → Visit **L**eft subtree → Visit **R**ight subtree.
- **Postorder Traversal (LRD):** Visit **L**eft subtree → Visit **R**ight subtree → Visit data (Root).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Photo Album

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you have a **family photo album** organized as a tree: your grandparents at the top, their children (your parents and uncles/aunts) in the middle row, and you and your cousins at the bottom.
>
> - **Inorder** is like a **genealogist** who records the family lineage from the *youngest descendant up to the eldest ancestor on the left*, then the ancestor, then the right lineage. → **Left → Root → Right**
> - **Preorder** is like a **photographer** who first takes a picture of the ancestor, *then* moves to the left descendants, *then* the right descendants. → **Root → Left → Right**
> - **Postorder** is like a **janitor cleaning up** — they first clean the leaves (descendants), *then* the children, and *finally* the ancestor at the top. → **Left → Right → Root**

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | Unit / Symbol |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Node** | A fundamental unit containing data and pointers to children | — |
| **Root** | The topmost node with no parent | Top of tree |
| **Leaf** | A node with no children (both pointers `NULL`) | Degree = 0 |
| **Height (h)** | The longest path from root to a leaf | **Edges** |
| **Depth** | Distance of a node from the root | **Edges** |
| **Subtree** | A node plus all its descendants | Recursive unit |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab record expects students to **manually create the tree** by inserting nodes, then display the traversal output with **proper labels** (e.g., `Inorder Traversal: 4 2 5 1 6 3 7`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Traversal

### 2.1.1 Inorder Traversal (Left – Root – Right)

- **Step 1:** Recursively traverse the **left subtree** until a `NULL` pointer is reached.
- **Step 2:** **Visit the root node** (print/process its data).
- **Step 3:** Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.
- **Why it matters:** Produces nodes in **non-decreasing sorted order** for a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**. This property is heavily tested in KTU university exams.

### 2.1.2 Preorder Traversal (Root – Left – Right)

- **Step 1:** **Visit the root node** first.
- **Step 2:** Recursively traverse the **left subtree**.
- **Step 3:** Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.
- **Why it matters:** Used to **create a copy of the tree** and to generate the **prefix expression** of an expression tree. The root is the first node visited.

### 2.1.3 Postorder Traversal (Left – Right – Root)

- **Step 1:** Recursively traverse the **left subtree**.
- **Step 2:** Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.
- **Step 3:** **Visit the root node** last.
- **Why it matters:** Used to **delete the tree** (children are freed before parent to prevent memory leaks) and to evaluate **postfix expressions**. The root is the last node visited.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU lab viva and university exams.

| Traversal Type | Visit Order | Time Complexity (T(n)) | Space Complexity (Auxiliary) | BST Sorted Output? | Common Application |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Inorder** | Left → Root → Right | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | **Yes** (Ascending) | BST sorting, Kth smallest |
| **Preorder** | Root → Left → Right | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | No | Tree cloning, Prefix expr. |
| **Postorder** | Left → Right → Root | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | No | Tree deletion, Postfix expr. |
| **Level Order** | Level by level (BFS) | $O(n)$ | $O(w)$ | No | Shortest path, BFS |

> **Notation Used:**
> - $n$ = total number of nodes in the tree
> - $h$ = height of the tree (worst case: $n-1$ for skewed, $\log_2 n$ for balanced)
> - $w$ = maximum width of the tree

## 2.3 Recurrence Relation & Complexity Derivation

For a balanced binary tree with $n$ nodes, the work done at each node is a **constant** $O(1)$ (one print statement), and the problem is divided into **two sub-problems** of size $n/2$ each (left and right subtrees).

The recurrence relation is:

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1)$$

Applying the **Master Theorem** with $a=2$, $b=2$, $f(n)=O(1)$:

$$\log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1$$

Since $f(n) = O(1) = O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1$, we fall under **Case 1** of the Master Theorem.

$$T(n) = O(n^{\log_b a}) = O(n^1) = O(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Tree traversals are the backbone of **compilers** (Abstract Syntax Tree traversal for code generation), **databases** (B-tree indexing), **file systems** (directory listing), and **AI search algorithms** (decision tree evaluation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Analysis & C Implementation

## 3.1 Sample Binary Tree for Trace Analysis

We will use the following binary tree as a **running example** throughout this section to demonstrate every traversal. This is the **standard KTU lab sample tree**.

```
                [ 1 ]            <-- Root
                /    \
            [ 2 ]    [ 3 ]
            /   \     /   \
         [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ]   <-- Leaves
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — INORDER (LDR)

**Rule:** Left → Root → Right

The recursive call chain with explicit **call stack push/pop** is shown below. Each indentation represents a deeper recursive call.

| Step | Call (Push) | Action | Output Buffer |
|------|-------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | `inorder(1)` | Go left | — |
| 2 | `inorder(2)` | Go left | — |
| 3 | `inorder(4)` | Go left → NULL, return | — |
| 4 | (Pop back to 4) | **Print 4** | `4` |
| 5 | (Pop back to 4) | Go right → NULL, return | `4` |
| 6 | (Pop back to 2) | **Print 2** | `4 2` |
| 7 | (Pop back to 2) | Go right | `4 2` |
| 8 | `inorder(5)` | Go left → NULL, return | `4 2` |
| 9 | (Pop back to 5) | **Print 5** | `4 2 5` |
| 10 | (Pop back to 5) | Go right → NULL, return | `4 2 5` |
| 11 | (Pop back to 1) | **Print 1** | `4 2 5 1` |
| 12 | (Pop back to 1) | Go right | `4 2 5 1` |
| 13 | `inorder(3)` | Go left | `4 2 5 1` |
| 14 | `inorder(6)` | Left NULL, **Print 6**, Right NULL | `4 2 5 1 6` |
| 15 | (Pop back to 3) | **Print 3** | `4 2 5 1 6 3` |
| 16 | (Pop back to 3) | Go right | `4 2 5 1 6 3` |
| 17 | `inorder(7)` | Left NULL, **Print 7**, Right NULL | `4 2 5 1 6 3 7` |

**Final Inorder Output:** `4 2 5 1 6 3 7`

> [!NOTE]
> If this tree were a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, notice the inorder output `4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 7` is **NOT sorted** — confirming the sample tree is **not a BST** but a general binary tree.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — PREORDER (DLR)

**Rule:** Root → Left → Right

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | **Print 1** (Root) | `1` |
| 2 | Go left → `preorder(2)` | `1` |
| 3 | **Print 2** | `1 2` |
| 4 | Go left → `preorder(4)` | `1 2` |
| 5 | **Print 4**, Left NULL, Right NULL | `1 2 4` |
| 6 | Back to 2 → Go right → `preorder(5)` | `1 2 4` |
| 7 | **Print 5**, Left NULL, Right NULL | `1 2 4 5` |
| 8 | Back to 1 → Go right → `preorder(3)` | `1 2 4 5` |
| 9 | **Print 3** | `1 2 4 5 3` |
| 10 | Go left → `preorder(6)` | `1 2 4 5 3` |
| 11 | **Print 6** | `1 2 4 5 3 6` |
| 12 | Go right → `preorder(7)` | `1 2 4 5 3 6` |
| 13 | **Print 7** | `1 2 4 5 3 6 7` |

**Final Preorder Output:** `1 2 4 5 3 6 7`

## 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — POSTORDER (LRD)

**Rule:** Left → Right → Root

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Go left from 1 → `postorder(2)` | — |
| 2 | Go left from 2 → `postorder(4)` | — |
| 3 | Left NULL, Right NULL, **Print 4** | `4` |
| 4 | Back to 2 → Go right → `postorder(5)` | `4` |
| 5 | Left NULL, Right NULL, **Print 5** | `4 5` |
| 6 | Back to 2 → **Print 2** | `4 5 2` |
| 7 | Back to 1 → Go right → `postorder(3)` | `4 5 2` |
| 8 | Go left → `postorder(6)` | `4 5 2` |
| 9 | Left NULL, Right NULL, **Print 6** | `4 5 2 6` |
| 10 | Go right → `postorder(7)` | `4 5 2 6` |
| 11 | Left NULL, Right NULL, **Print 7** | `4 5 2 6 7` |
| 12 | Back to 3 → **Print 3** | `4 5 2 6 7 3` |
| 13 | Back to 1 → **Print 1** | `4 5 2 6 7 3 1` |

**Final Postorder Output:** `4 5 2 6 7 3 1`

## 3.5 Complete C Program — KTU Lab Standard (Recursive Implementation)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

// Step 1: Define the node structure
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* left;
    struct Node* right;
};

// Step 2: Function to create a new node dynamically
struct Node* createNode(int value) {
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->left = NULL;
    newNode->right = NULL;
    return newNode;
}

// Step 3a: Inorder Traversal (Left - Root - Right)
void inorder(struct Node* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    inorder(root->left);
    printf("%d ", root->data);
    inorder(root->right);
}

// Step 3b: Preorder Traversal (Root - Left - Right)
void preorder(struct Node* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    printf("%d ", root->data);
    preorder(root->left);
    preorder(root->right);
}

// Step 3c: Postorder Traversal (Left - Right - Root)
void postorder(struct Node* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    postorder(root->left);
    postorder(root->right);
    printf("%d ", root->data);
}

int main() {
    // Manually constructing the binary tree
    struct Node* root = createNode(1);
    root->left  = createNode(2);
    root->right = createNode(3);
    root->left->left  = createNode(4);
    root->left->right = createNode(5);
    root->right->left = createNode(6);
    root->right->right = createNode(7);

    printf("Inorder   Traversal: ");
    inorder(root);
    printf("\n");

    printf("Preorder  Traversal: ");
    preorder(root);
    printf("\n");

    printf("Postorder Traversal: ");
    postorder(root);
    printf("\n");

    return 0;
}
```

### 3.5.1 Expected Output

```
Inorder   Traversal: 4 2 5 1 6 3 7
Preorder  Traversal: 1 2 4 5 3 6 7
Postorder Traversal: 4 5 2 6 7 3 1
```

## 3.6 Mathematical Recurrence for Time Complexity

The total work $T(n)$ for any traversal on a tree of $n$ nodes is:

$$T(n) = T(L) + T(R) + O(1)$$

where $L$ is the number of nodes in the left subtree and $R$ is the number of nodes in the right subtree, with $L + R + 1 = n$.

For a **balanced** tree, $L \approx R \approx n/2$:

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

Expanding recursively:

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c\right] + c = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 3c$$

At the $k$-th level of expansion (tree has $\log_2 n$ levels):

$$T(n) = 2^k T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + (2^k - 1)c$$

At the base case, $k = \log_2 n$ and $T(1) = c$:

$$T(n) = n \cdot c + (n - 1)c = O(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The time complexity $O(n)$ is **independent** of the tree's shape (skewed or balanced). Every traversal visits each node **exactly once**, contributing one unit of work per node.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Traversal System

For a deeper architectural understanding, the following block diagram maps the **complete interaction** between the user, the C program modules, and the system call stack during traversal.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[USER INPUT - Tree Construction] --> B[MAIN FUNCTION]
    B --> C[createNode FUNCTION - malloc]
    C --> D[HEAP MEMORY - Node Objects]
    B --> E[inorder CALL]
    B --> F[preorder CALL]
    B --> G[postorder CALL]
    E --> H[CALL STACK - Recursive Frames]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[BASE CASE CHECK - root equals NULL]
    I -->|False| J[RECURSIVE CALL - Left and Right]
    I -->|True| K[RETURN to Caller]
    J --> L[VISIT NODE - printf statement]
    L --> M[OUTPUT - Console Display]
    K --> M
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style H fill:#FFA07A
    style L fill:#90EE90
    style M fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 ASCII Tree Diagram (Lab Record Reference)

For students who need to draw this tree in their **physical lab record**, use the following clean ASCII representation:

```

                /   \
              2       3
             / \     / \
            4   5   6   7
```

| Position in Tree | Traversal that Visits It First |
|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Root (1) | **Preorder** |
| Innermost Left Leaf (4) | **Inorder** & **Postorder** |
| Innermost Right Leaf (7) | **Inorder** & **Postorder** |
| Last Visited Node | **Postorder** (Root 1 is last) |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define binary tree traversal. List any three depth-first traversals with their visit order. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **binary tree traversal** is the process of visiting each node in a binary tree **exactly once** in a systematic order. The three depth-first traversals are:

1. **Inorder (LDR):** Left subtree → Root → Right subtree
2. **Preorder (DLR):** Root → Left subtree → Right subtree
3. **Postorder (LRD):** Left subtree → Right subtree → Root

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing with correct visit order.)*

### Q2. Why is the inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree (BST) always sorted? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, for every node:
- All nodes in the **left subtree** have values **less than** the node's value.
- All nodes in the **right subtree** have values **greater than** the node's value.

Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order **Left → Root → Right**. Therefore:
- All left-subtree values (smaller) are printed first.
- The root is printed next.
- All right-subtree values (larger) are printed last.

This naturally produces nodes in **non-decreasing (ascending) sorted order**, with **$O(n)$ time complexity** and no extra comparison logic needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** `if (root == NULL) return;` — this causes **infinite recursion** and **segmentation fault**. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up the order** of L, D, R in the three traversals — examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for correct order.
> 3. **Not using `malloc` / dynamic allocation** — KTU lab strictly requires heap-based node creation, not static arrays. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to include `<stdlib.h>`** — compilation error, no marks for output.
> 5. **Failing to print traversal labels** (e.g., "Inorder:") — loss of **0.5 mark** for presentation.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Write a C program to construct the following binary tree dynamically and display all three recursive traversals (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder). **(7 marks)**

```
                10
               /  \
             20    30
            /  \      \
          40    50    60
```

**(b)** Trace the execution of the **Preorder traversal** step-by-step, showing the call stack push/pop operations and the final output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* left;
    struct Node* right;
};

struct Node* createNode(int v) {
    struct Node* n = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    n->data = v;
    n->left = n->right = NULL;
    return n;
}

void inorder(struct Node* r)   { if(r){inorder(r->left); printf("%d ",r->data); inorder(r->right);} }
void preorder(struct Node* r)  { if(r){printf("%d ",r->data); preorder(r->left); preorder(r->right);} }
void postorder(struct Node* r) { if(r){postorder(r->left); postorder(r->right); printf("%d ",r->data);} }

int main() {
    struct Node* root = createNode(10);
    root->left = createNode(20);
    root->right = createNode(30);
    root->left->left = createNode(40);
    root->left->right = createNode(50);
    root->right->right = createNode(60);

    printf("Inorder: ");   inorder(root);   printf("\n");
    printf("Preorder: ");  preorder(root);  printf("\n");
    printf("Postorder: "); postorder(root); printf("\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Inorder:   40 20 50 10 30 60
Preorder:  10 20 40 50 30 60
Postorder: 40 50 20 60 30 10
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Step | Stack (Top at Right) | Action | Output |
|------|----------------------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `[preorder(10)]` | PUSH root 10, **Print 10** | `10` |
| 2 | `[preorder(10), preorder(20)]` | PUSH left child 20, **Print 20** | `10 20` |
| 3 | `[preorder(10), preorder(20), preorder(40)]` | PUSH left-left 40, **Print 40** | `10 20 40` |
| 4 | `[preorder(10), preorder(20)]` | POP 40 (left=NULL, right=NULL) | `10 20 40` |
| 5 | `[preorder(10), preorder(20), preorder(50)]` | PUSH right of 20 = 50, **Print 50** | `10 20 40 50` |
| 6 | `[preorder(10), preorder(20)]` | POP 50 | `10 20 40 50` |
| 7 | `[preorder(10)]` | POP 20 (both children done) | `10 20 40 50` |
| 8 | `[preorder(10), preorder(30)]` | PUSH right of 10 = 30, **Print 30** | `10 20 40 50 30` |
| 9 | `[preorder(10), preorder(30)]` | Left of 30 is NULL, skip | `10 20 40 50 30` |
| 10 | `[preorder(10), preorder(30), preorder(60)]` | PUSH right of 30 = 60, **Print 60** | `10 20 40 50 30 60` |
| 11 | `[preorder(10), preorder(30)]` | POP 60 | `10 20 40 50 30 60` |
| 12 | `[]` | POP 30, POP 10 — traversal complete | `10 20 40 50 30 60` |

**Final Preorder Output:** `10 20 40 50 30 60`

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder traversals in terms of **visit order, BST property, time complexity, and a real-world application**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the expression tree below, write the **Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder** traversals and identify which one gives the **infix, prefix, and postfix** forms of the expression. **(7 marks)**

```
                *
               / \
              +   -
             / \ / \
            3  4 5  2
```
*(Expression: (3 + 4) * (5 - 2))*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Criterion | Inorder (LDR) | Preorder (DLR) | Postorder (LRD) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Visit Order** | Left → Root → Right | Root → Left → Right | Left → Right → Root |
| **Root Position** | Middle (2nd) | First | Last |
| **BST Sorted Output** | **Yes** (ascending) | No | No |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Application** | BST → sorted list | Tree cloning, prefix expr. | Tree deletion, postfix expr. |
| **Used By** | Compilers (infix) | Directory listing | Stack machine evaluation |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

- **Inorder Traversal (LDR):** `3 + 4 * 5 - 2` → This is the **INFIX** form (with parentheses: $(3+4)*(5-2)$).
- **Preorder Traversal (DLR):** `* + 3 4 - 5 2` → This is the **PREFIX** form (Polish notation).
- **Postorder Traversal (LRD):** `3 4 + 5 2 - *` → This is the **POSTFIX** form (Reverse Polish Notation — RPN).

**Identification (1 mark each):**
- **Inorder** → Infix
- **Preorder** → Prefix
- **Postorder** → Postfix

> **Note:** A stack-based evaluator processes the **postfix** form `3 4 + 5 2 - *` using only one pass and one stack, without needing parentheses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**

- 🔹 A **binary tree traversal** visits every node **exactly once**.
- 🔹 **Inorder (LDR):** Left → Root → Right → produces **sorted output for BSTs**.
- 🔹 **Preorder (DLR):** Root → Left → Right → root is **visited first**; used for **cloning**.
- 🔹 **Postorder (LRD):** Left → Right → Root → root is **visited last**; used for **deletion**.
- 🔹 Every traversal has **time complexity $O(n)$** and **auxiliary space $O(h)$** due to recursion stack.
- 🔹 The **base case** `if (root == NULL) return;` is **mandatory** — without it, **infinite recursion** occurs.
- 🔹 Use `malloc()` for **dynamic node creation** — KTU lab requires heap memory, not static arrays.
- 🔹 Always **include `<stdlib.h>`** for `malloc` and `<stdio.h>` for `printf`.
- 🔹 **Inorder** of an expression tree = **Infix**, **Preorder** = **Prefix**, **Postorder** = **Postfix**.
- 🔹 The **first node** in Preorder = **Root** of the tree; the **last node** in Postorder = **Root**.
- 🔹 For the sample tree `1(2(4,5),3(6,7))`: Inorder=`4 2 5 1 6 3 7`, Preorder=`1 2 4 5 3 6 7`, Postorder=`4 5 2 6 7 3 1`.
- 🔹 In KTU lab viva, expect the question: *"Which traversal is used to delete a tree?"* → Answer: **Postorder** (deletes children before parent).
- 🔹 Skewed tree (worst case) has $h = n-1$ → recursion stack depth = $O(n)$ → risk of **stack overflow** for very deep trees.
- 🔹 Iterative versions use an **explicit stack** (and a `visited` flag for postorder) to avoid recursion overhead.

topics.title: Implementation of Sorting Algorithms - Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort and Selection Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Sorting Algorithms - Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort and Selection Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sorting** is the fundamental computational operation of rearranging the elements of a list (or array) into a specific order — most commonly numerical (ascending/descending) or lexicographical (alphabetical). In the context of **Data Structures Lab (PCEOL308)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, sorting algorithms are classified as **comparison-based, in-place internal sorting algorithms** when the data fits entirely within the main memory (RAM).

The three sorting algorithms covered in **Module Experiment 2** are:

| Algorithm | Classification | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Bubble Sort** | Comparison-based, Stable, In-place | Adjacent element swapping (Exchange sort) |
| **Insertion Sort** | Comparison-based, Stable, In-place | Incremental insertion into a sorted sub-array |
| **Selection Sort** | Comparison-based, Unstable, In-place | Repeated selection of minimum/maximum element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module Experiment 2 explicitly tests the student's ability to *implement*, *trace*, and *analyze* the time complexity of these three elementary sorting algorithms using the C programming language. Questions in the **University End-Semester Exam (ESE)** frequently ask for hand-tracing, complexity analysis, and identifying the sorted output for a given unsorted input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Let us demystify these algorithms with a simple classroom analogy:

### 🎈 Bubble Sort — "The Bubbling Effect"
Imagine a vertical tube filled with water and **air bubbles of different sizes** trapped at the bottom. If you shake the tube gently, the **smaller bubbles (lighter elements)** will gradually rise to the top because they are lighter, while the **larger bubbles (heavier elements)** sink to the bottom. Each "shake" causes a swap between **adjacent** bubbles. This is exactly how Bubble Sort works: in every pass, the **largest unsorted element "bubbles up"** to its correct position at the end of the array.

### 🃏 Insertion Sort — "The Card Player's Hand"
Think of a card player who **picks up playing cards one by one** from a deck. As each new card arrives, the player slides it into its correct position among the cards already held in their hand (which are always sorted). The left hand maintains a **sorted sub-array**, and the right hand picks a **new "key" element** to insert. This is the precise mental model of Insertion Sort.

### 🎯 Selection Sort — "The Talent Show Judge"
Imagine a judge at a talent show who scans through all contestants, finds the **best performer (minimum element)**, and places that person in the first position. The judge then searches the *remaining* contestants for the next best, placing them in the second position, and so on. Selection Sort **selects** the minimum element from the unsorted portion and **swaps** it with the element at the current boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All three algorithms share a time complexity of **O(n²)** in the **worst and average cases**, but they differ in their **best-case behavior** and **stability** properties. Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort can achieve **O(n)** in the best case (already sorted input), while Selection Sort always remains **O(n²)**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **In-place algorithm:** Uses only **O(1)** auxiliary memory (constant extra space beyond the input array).
- **Stable sort:** Preserves the **relative order** of equal elements. Critical for sorting records by multiple keys.
- **Time complexity:** Measured as the number of **key comparisons** and **swaps/assignments**.
- **Adaptive algorithm:** Performance improves when the input is **partially sorted**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm 1: Bubble Sort — Operational Logic

Bubble Sort is the simplest comparison-based sorting algorithm. It works by **repeatedly stepping through the list**, comparing adjacent pairs, and swapping them if they are in the wrong order.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. Start from index `0` and compare `arr[i]` with `arr[i+1]`.
2. If `arr[i] > arr[i+1]`, **swap** them (push the larger element rightward).
3. After one complete pass, the **largest element** will have "bubbled" to the end of the array.
4. Repeat the process for the remaining `n-1`, then `n-2`, ... elements.
5. **Optimization:** Use a `swapped` flag. If no swap occurs in a pass, the array is already sorted — **terminate early**.

### Optimized C Implementation:
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, temp, swapped;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        swapped = 0;
        for (j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) {
                temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
                swapped = 1;
            }
        }
        if (swapped == 0)
            break;
    }
}
```

## 2.2 Algorithm 2: Insertion Sort — Operational Logic

Insertion Sort builds the final sorted array **one element at a time**. It picks an element from the unsorted region and inserts it into its correct position in the sorted region.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. Assume the first element (`arr[0]`) is already a **sorted sub-array of size 1**.
2. For each `i` from `1` to `n-1`, take `arr[i]` as the **key**.
3. Compare the key with elements `arr[i-1], arr[i-2], ...` in the sorted region.
4. **Shift** all elements greater than the key one position to the right.
5. Insert the key at the **vacated position**.
6. After each iteration, the sorted region grows by one element.

### C Implementation:
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void insertionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, key;
    for (i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        key = arr[i];
        j = i - 1;
        while (j >= 0 && arr[j] > key) {
            arr[j + 1] = arr[j];
            j = j - 1;
        }
        arr[j + 1] = key;
    }
}
```

## 2.3 Algorithm 3: Selection Sort — Operational Logic

Selection Sort divides the array into two regions: a **sorted region on the left** and an **unsorted region on the right**. It repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted region and places it at the boundary of the sorted region.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. For each position `i` from `0` to `n-2`:
2. Find the **index of the minimum element** in the unsorted region `arr[i+1...n-1]`.
3. **Swap** `arr[i]` with the element at that minimum index.
4. The sorted region now grows to size `i+1`.

### C Implementation:
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void selectionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, minIdx, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        minIdx = i;
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < arr[minIdx]) {
                minIdx = j;
            }
        }
        temp = arr[minIdx];
        arr[minIdx] = arr[i];
        arr[i] = temp;
    }
}
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Bubble Sort | Insertion Sort | Selection Sort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best Case Time** | $O(n)$ (optimized) | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Average Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Worst Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Stable?** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** |
| **Adaptive?** | **Yes** (with flag) | **Yes** | **No** |
| **In-place?** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Number of Swaps (Worst)** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Use Case** | Nearly sorted data | Small / streaming data | Minimum swaps required |

### Derivation of Worst-Case Comparisons:

For all three algorithms, the **outer loop** runs for $n-1$ iterations. The **inner loop** comparison count $C(n)$ for the worst case is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n-1-i) \\[4pt]
&= (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \ldots + 1 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{n^2 - n}{2} \\[4pt]
&= O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Selection Sort is preferred in embedded systems with **write-constrained memory** (e.g., EEPROM/Flash) because it performs at most $n-1$ swaps. Insertion Sort is used in **online sorting** scenarios where data arrives one element at a time. Bubble Sort, while pedagogically important, is rarely used in production systems due to its poor cache performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Trace Bubble Sort on `arr = [5, 1, 4, 2, 8]`

We will perform an **exhaustive hand-trace** (this is the most frequently asked KTU question pattern).

| Pass | Comparison (i, j) | Action | Array State After Swap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pass 1** | (0,1): 5 vs 1 | Swap | `[1, 5, 4, 2, 8]` |
|  | (1,2): 5 vs 4 | Swap | `[1, 4, 5, 2, 8]` |
|  | (2,3): 5 vs 2 | Swap | `[1, 4, 2, 5, 8]` |
|  | (3,4): 5 vs 8 | No swap | `[1, 4, 2, 5, 8]` |
| **Pass 2** | (0,1): 1 vs 4 | No swap | `[1, 4, 2, 5, 8]` |
|  | (1,2): 4 vs 2 | Swap | `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]` |
|  | (2,3): 4 vs 5 | No swap | `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]` |
| **Pass 3** | (0,1): 1 vs 2 | No swap | `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]` |
|  | (1,2): 2 vs 4 | No swap | `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]` |
| **Pass 4** | (0,1): 1 vs 2 | No swap | `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]` |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]`

**Verification of total comparisons:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (n-1) + (n-2) + \ldots + 1 \\[4pt]
T(5) &= 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 \text{ comparisons}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Trace Insertion Sort on `arr = [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]`

| Iteration (i) | Key | Sorted Sub-array Evolution | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|
| i = 1 | 11 | Shift 12 right; insert 11 at 0 | `[11, 12, 13, 5, 6]` |
| i = 2 | 13 | 13 stays in place | `[11, 12, 13, 5, 6]` |
| i = 3 | 5 | Shift 13, 12, 11 right; insert 5 at 0 | `[5, 11, 12, 13, 6]` |
| i = 4 | 6 | Shift 13, 12, 11 right; insert 6 at 1 | `[5, 6, 11, 12, 13]` |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[5, 6, 11, 12, 13]`

## 3.3 Worked Example: Trace Selection Sort on `arr = [29, 10, 14, 37, 13]`

| Pass (i) | Min Found at Index | Swap Action | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|
| i = 0 | 1 (value 10) | Swap arr[0] ↔ arr[1] | `[10, 29, 14, 37, 13]` |
| i = 1 | 4 (value 13) | Swap arr[1] ↔ arr[4] | `[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]` |
| i = 2 | 2 (value 14) | No swap (already at boundary) | `[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]` |
| i = 3 | 4 (value 29) | Swap arr[3] ↔ arr[4] | `[10, 13, 14, 29, 37]` |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[10, 13, 14, 29, 37]`

## 3.4 Stability Demonstration — Why Selection Sort is Unstable

> [!WARNING]
> **Classic KTU Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that all in-place sorts are unstable. Selection Sort is the **counter-example** that examiners love to test.

Consider the input: `arr = [4a, 3, 4b, 1]` (where `4a` and `4b` have the same key value `4`).

- **Pass 1:** Minimum is `1` (index 3). Swap `arr[0]` ↔ `arr[3]`: `[1, 3, 4b, 4a]`
- **Pass 2:** Minimum is `3` (index 1). No swap.
- **Pass 3:** Minimum is `4b` (index 2). Swap `arr[2]` ↔ `arr[3]`: `[1, 3, 4a, 4b]`

Wait — the original order of `4a` before `4b` was **disturbed** (in Pass 1, `4b` moved before `4a`). Therefore, **stability is violated** at the *first* pass. This confirms Selection Sort is **not stable**.

## 3.5 Derivation: Total Number of Swaps in Selection Sort (Worst Case)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 1 = (n-1) \text{ swaps} \\[4pt]
S(n) &= n - 1 = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **linear swap count** that makes Selection Sort attractive in **write-limited hardware** environments.

## 3.6 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Bubble Sort | Insertion Sort | Selection Sort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer Loop Range** | `i = 0` to `n-2` | `i = 1` to `n-1` | `i = 0` to `n-2` |
| **Inner Loop Action** | Adjacent compare & swap | Shift & insert | Find minimum index |
| **Best Case Input** | Already sorted (flag = 0) | Already sorted (no shifts) | Any input (no early exit) |
| **Worst Case Input** | Reverse sorted | Reverse sorted | Any (always O(n²)) |
| **Comparisons (Worst)** | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bubble Sort — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize i = 0]
    B --> C{i < n - 1?}
    C -- No --> Z([End - Array Sorted])
    C -- Yes --> D[Initialize swapped = 0, j = 0]
    D --> E{j < n - i - 1?}
    E -- No --> F{swapped == 0?}
    F -- Yes --> Z
    F -- No --> G[i = i + 1]
    G --> C
    E -- Yes --> H{arr j > arr j+1 ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Swap arr j and arr j+1]
    I --> J[Set swapped = 1]
    J --> K[j = j + 1]
    H -- No --> K
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Insertion Sort — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize i = 1]
    B --> C{i < n?}
    C -- No --> Z([End - Array Sorted])
    C -- Yes --> D[key = arr i, j = i - 1]
    D --> E{j >= 0 AND arr j > key?}
    E -- No --> F[arr j+1 = key]
    F --> G[i = i + 1]
    G --> C
    E -- Yes --> H[arr j+1 = arr j]
    H --> I[j = j - 1]
    I --> E
```

## 4.3 Selection Sort — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize i = 0]
    B --> C{i < n - 1?}
    C -- No --> Z([End - Array Sorted])
    C -- Yes --> D[minIdx = i, j = i + 1]
    D --> E{j < n?}
    E -- No --> F[Swap arr i and arr minIdx]
    F --> G[i = i + 1]
    G --> C
    E -- Yes --> F1{arr j < arr minIdx?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[minIdx = j]
    G1 --> H[j = j + 1]
    F1 -- No --> H
    H --> E
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Sort Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        UA[Unsorted Array]
    end
    subgraph ALGO[Algorithm Selection Layer]
        B1[Bubble Sort Module]
        I1[Insertion Sort Module]
        S1[Selection Sort Module]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[Processing Engine]
        COMP[Comparison Logic]
        SWAP[Swap Logic]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        SA[Sorted Array]
        STAB[Stability Flag]
        COMP2[Complexity Report]
    end
    UA --> B1
    UA --> I1
    UA --> S1
    B1 --> COMP
    I1 --> COMP
    S1 --> COMP
    COMP --> SWAP
    SWAP --> SA
    B1 --> STAB
    I1 --> STAB
    S1 --> STAB
    B1 --> COMP2
    I1 --> COMP2
    S1 --> COMP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows that all three algorithms share a common **Comparison Logic** and **Swap Logic** block, but differ in their **outer-loop control flow** and **boundary management**. This is why all three are classified as **comparison-based** sorting algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define sorting. List any two comparison-based sorting algorithms and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Sorting** is the process of arranging the elements of a list/array in a specific order (ascending or descending). Two comparison-based sorting algorithms are:
>
> 1. **Bubble Sort** — Advantage: *Adaptive*; in the best case (already sorted), it runs in $O(n)$ time when implemented with a swap flag.
> 2. **Insertion Sort** — Advantage: *Stable* and performs exceptionally well on small or nearly sorted datasets ($O(n)$ best case).
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for algorithms, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between stable and unstable sorting algorithms. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **stable sorting algorithm** preserves the relative order of records with equal keys. An **unstable sorting algorithm** may change the relative order of equal-keyed elements.
>
> - **Stable Example:** Bubble Sort or Insertion Sort.
> - **Unstable Example:** Selection Sort.
>
> For instance, sorting `[4a, 3, 4b, 1]` with Selection Sort produces `[1, 3, 4a, 4b]`, violating the original order of the two `4`s.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for demonstration.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Write a complete C program to sort an array of `n` integers in ascending order using the **Bubble Sort** algorithm. Trace your algorithm on the input: `arr = [42, 15, 8, 23, 4, 16]` and show the array state after every pass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyze the **time complexity** of Bubble Sort in the best, average, and worst cases. Explain why the **optimized version** (with swap flag) is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**C Program (4 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, temp, swapped;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        swapped = 0;
        for (j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) {
                temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
                swapped = 1;
            }
        }
        if (swapped == 0)
            break;
    }
}

int main() {
    int arr[] = {42, 15, 8, 23, 4, 16};
    int n = 6, i;
    bubbleSort(arr, n);
    printf("Sorted Array: ");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    return 0;
}
```

**Hand-Trace (3 marks):**

| Pass | Array State After Pass |
|---|---|
| Initial | `[42, 15, 8, 23, 4, 16]` |
| Pass 1 | `[15, 8, 23, 4, 16, 42]` |
| Pass 2 | `[8, 15, 4, 16, 23, 42]` |
| Pass 3 | `[8, 4, 15, 16, 23, 42]` |
| Pass 4 | `[4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42]` |
| Pass 5 | `swapped = 0` → terminate early |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42]`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Time Complexity Analysis (5 marks):**

| Case | Condition | Comparisons | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best** | Already sorted input | $n - 1$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Average** | Random input | $\frac{n(n-1)}{4}$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Worst** | Reverse sorted input | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $O(n^2)$ |

**Derivation of Worst Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n-1-i) \\[4pt]
&= (n-1) + (n-2) + \ldots + 1 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Optimized Version is Preferred (2 marks):**
> In the basic Bubble Sort, the algorithm **always** executes all $(n-1)$ passes, even if the array gets sorted in the middle. The optimized version uses a **`swapped` flag** to detect that no swap occurred in a particular pass (meaning the array is already sorted) and **breaks out of the loop early**. This reduces the best-case time complexity from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$, making it an **adaptive algorithm**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Implement the **Insertion Sort** algorithm in C. Sort the array `[34, 12, 45, 6, 23, 11]` and display the sorted output. Show the array state after each outer-loop iteration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the **Selection Sort** algorithm in C for the same input. Compare the number of **swaps** performed by Selection Sort versus Insertion Sort on this input. Which is more efficient in terms of memory writes? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**C Program (4 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void insertionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, key;
    for (i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        key = arr[i];
        j = i - 1;
        while (j >= 0 && arr[j] > key) {
            arr[j + 1] = arr[j];
            j--;
        }
        arr[j + 1] = key;
    }
}

int main() {
    int arr[] = {34, 12, 45, 6, 23, 11};
    int n = 6, i;
    insertionSort(arr, n);
    printf("Sorted Array: ");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    return 0;
}
```

**Hand-Trace (3 marks):**

| Iteration (i) | Key | Array State |
|---|---|---|
| i = 1 | 12 | `[12, 34, 45, 6, 23, 11]` |
| i = 2 | 45 | `[12, 34, 45, 6, 23, 11]` |
| i = 3 | 6 | `[6, 12, 34, 45, 23, 11]` |
| i = 4 | 23 | `[6, 12, 23, 34, 45, 11]` |
| i = 5 | 11 | `[6, 11, 12, 23, 34, 45]` |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[6, 11, 12, 23, 34, 45]`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**C Program for Selection Sort (3 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

void selectionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, minIdx, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        minIdx = i;
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < arr[minIdx]) {
                minIdx = j;
            }
        }
        temp = arr[minIdx];
        arr[minIdx] = arr[i];
        arr[i] = temp;
    }
}
```

**Swap Count Comparison (4 marks):**

| Algorithm | Number of Swaps on `[34, 12, 45, 6, 23, 11]` |
|---|---|
| **Insertion Sort** | **Many shifts** — each iteration may shift multiple elements. Total memory writes $\approx O(n^2)$. |
| **Selection Sort** | Exactly $(n-1) = 5$ swaps — one swap per outer iteration. |

**Conclusion:**
> **Selection Sort is more efficient in terms of memory writes** because it performs exactly $(n-1)$ swaps regardless of input distribution, while Insertion Sort may perform $O(n^2)$ shifts in the worst case. This makes Selection Sort preferable in **memory-write-constrained environments** such as EEPROM, Flash storage, or hardware with limited write cycles.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing `j < n - i - 1` with `j < n - 1`** in Bubble Sort — this causes one extra pass and an **out-of-bounds memory access**. Always terminate the inner loop at `n - i - 1` to skip the already-sorted suffix.
> 2. **Forgetting the `swapped` flag in optimized Bubble Sort** — the examiner will deduct 1 mark if the optimized version is requested but not implemented.
> 3. **Off-by-one error in Insertion Sort:** The outer loop must start at `i = 1` (not `i = 0`), because the first element is trivially "sorted."
> 4. **Incorrect stability claim:** Stating that Selection Sort is stable is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. Always back the claim with a counter-example trace.
> 5. **Missing the `j >= 0` check** in Insertion Sort's `while` loop — this causes **segmentation faults** for the smallest key value.
> 6. **Confusing swaps vs. comparisons:** KTU questions often ask for the **swap count** specifically, not the comparison count. Read the question stem carefully.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bubble Sort** is a **stable, adaptive, in-place** sort with $O(n)$ best case and $O(n^2)$ worst case.
- ✅ **Insertion Sort** is **stable, adaptive, in-place**, and excels on small / nearly-sorted inputs. Best case is $O(n)$.
- ✅ **Selection Sort** is **unstable, non-adaptive, in-place**, and always $O(n^2)$, but performs only $(n-1)$ swaps — ideal for write-limited hardware.
- ✅ All three algorithms use **$O(1)$ auxiliary space** and are classified as **comparison-based internal sorts**.
- ✅ The worst-case comparison count for all three is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$.
- ✅ **Stability** matters when sorting records by secondary keys (e.g., sort students by marks, then by name alphabetically).
- ✅ The **outer loop boundary** differs: Bubble and Selection use `i = 0` to `n-2`; Insertion uses `i = 1` to `n-1`.
- ✅ **Always trace** with a small example (n = 5 or 6) when solving KTU theory questions — examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the hand-trace table.
